question: Please introduce yourself.
context: Keep it to about one minute. Speak slowly. You do not need to tell your full life story.
shortAnswer: I am a Java backend developer. I have worked for many years with business systems. My main skills are Java, Jakarta EE, SQL, PostgreSQL and system integrations. I also build personal projects with Spring Boot and Angular. Now I want to grow with Kotlin, Kafka and cloud tools in a real team.
detailedAnswer: Use four simple parts: who you are, your main skills, one type of work you know well, and why this role is your next step. Stop after one minute and let the interviewer ask questions. Do not claim production experience with tools that you are still learning.
keyPoints: Be honest about what you are learning
redFlags: Claiming experience you do not have

followUps.question: What project are you most proud of?
Which skill would help this team first?

question: Why are you interested in this REWE Digital position?
shortAnswer: I like this role because it mixes Java development, real business problems and logistics. I have experience with enterprise Java, databases and integrations. I can help with these areas, and I also want to learn more about Kotlin, Kafka and Kubernetes. I like teams that improve existing systems and build new features.
detailedAnswer: Connect your answer to the job description. Say what you can bring today and what you want to learn next. It is good to say that you are learning a technology; it is not good to pretend that you already know the team's internal systems.
redFlags: Giving the same answer for every company
Saying only that you want to learn new tools

followUps.question: What can you contribute in your first months?
What would you like to learn from this role?

question: What do you understand about the TRAB team?
shortAnswer: From the job description, I understand that TRAB builds software for transport logistics. The work supports distribution to many REWE supermarkets. The team works with web applications, APIs, backend services and integrations. I would like to learn which business flow and system I would work on first.
detailedAnswer: Say only what is in the job description. Then name one or two things you would ask. This shows preparation and honesty. Do not invent details about the architecture, traffic or daily work.
keyPoints: Ask about the first area of work
redFlags: Talking as if you already know their architecture

followUps.question: What would you like to understand first?
Which part of the job description interests you most?

question: How would you handle duplicate or late transport events?
shortAnswer: First, I would understand the business rule. Then I would give each event an ID and make processing safe to repeat. I would check the current status before changing it. If an event is too old or invalid, I keep it for review instead of silently changing the data.
detailedAnswer: Use simple words first: every event needs an ID, the service must be safe when it receives the same event twice, and the system must not move a delivery back to an old status. Then mention logs, metrics and a process to review failed events. This is a design approach, not a claim about TRAB's current system.
redFlags: Saying the message broker solves every problem

followUps.question: What happens when the database is unavailable?
How would you monitor failed events?

question: What do you know about NEO?
shortAnswer: I know from the vacancy that NEO is a communication platform with a modular design. I do not know the details yet. I would ask who uses it, what problem it solves, how modules connect and whether it is already running in production. I prefer to understand the problem before suggesting a solution.
detailedAnswer: This is a good answer because it is honest. Words such as modular and plug-and-play can mean many things. Ask about users, integrations, data, security, deployment and the main problems. Do not guess that it uses a specific framework or broker.
keyPoints: State what you do not know

followUps.question: Which question would you ask first?
How would you test a module safely?

question: Which of your skills transfer to this role?
shortAnswer: My main transferable skills are Java, SQL performance, PostgreSQL, integrations and asynchronous processing with JMS. I know how to work carefully in important business systems and how to improve legacy code step by step. My production experience is mainly Jakarta EE and JMS. Spring Boot and Angular are hands-on project experience, and Kotlin, Kafka and Kubernetes are areas I am growing in.
detailedAnswer: Be clear about the difference between production experience, project experience and study. You can say that messaging concepts transfer from JMS to Kafka, but you still need to learn Kafka-specific practices. This makes your answer credible.
redFlags: Saying JMS and Kafka are the same
Calling a personal project production experience

followUps.question: What would you need to learn first?
Which messaging concept transfers from JMS to Kafka?

question: What questions would you ask us at the end?
shortAnswer: I would ask. What would be my first project? What is the biggest problem the team has today? How do you measure success in this role? How does the team make technical decisions? These questions help me understand the real work.
detailedAnswer: Choose two or three questions, not ten. Listen during the interview and ask about something that was not explained. Good topics are the first project, the team's current problem, on-call work, technical decisions and success after six months.

followUps.question: Which question matters most to you?
What would you ask about success in this role?

question: Why should we hire you?
shortAnswer: I bring strong experience with Java business systems, databases and integrations. I work carefully and I like to understand the real problem before I change code. I can contribute with my experience, learn the team's domain and grow with the technologies used here.
detailedAnswer: Give three reasons only: relevant skills, your way of working, and your motivation for the role. Do not say that you are the best candidate. Show what you can do and let your examples support it.

followUps.question: What would you improve first in a new system?
How do you make a change safely?

question: What are your main strengths?
shortAnswer: One strength is problem solving. I like to look at logs, data and the real business flow before I change something. Another strength is working carefully with existing systems. I also communicate clearly when I need help or when I see a risk.
detailedAnswer: Choose two or three strengths that match the role. For each strength, prepare one real example. Simple and specific is better than a long list of positive words.
redFlags: Using a strength with no example

followUps.question: Can you give an example?
How do these strengths help a team?

question: What is one weakness you are working on?
shortAnswer: Sometimes I spend too much time trying to solve a problem alone. I am improving this by sharing the problem earlier, with clear information about what I tried. This helps the team make decisions faster.
detailedAnswer: Choose a real weakness that is not essential for the role. Say what you are doing to improve it. Do not use a fake weakness such as 'I work too hard'. Be honest, brief and practical.
redFlags: Giving a fake strength as a weakness

followUps.question: How do you know you are improving?
When do you ask a teammate for help?

question: Tell me about a difficult problem you solved.
shortAnswer: In one system, a request was very slow because it made many database queries. I checked the logs and SQL, found the main problem and changed the query design. The request became much faster. I learned to measure first and change one thing at a time.
detailedAnswer: Use STAR: Situation, Task, Action, Result. Keep each part short. You can use your real SGN3 performance example: a request over 26,705 records took about 16 minutes and made around 48,000 queries; after query and pagination improvements, it took about two seconds with roughly ten queries. Use numbers only if you can explain them.

followUps.question: How did you verify the improvement?
What would you do differently next time?

question: How do you work in a team?
shortAnswer: I try to be clear, respectful and reliable. I share progress and risks early. When I do not understand something, I ask questions. I like code review because it improves the code and helps the team learn together.
detailedAnswer: The interviewer wants to know how you communicate, not whether you work alone. Give a short answer, then be ready with a real example of collaboration, review, planning or helping another developer.
redFlags: Saying you prefer to work alone

followUps.question: Tell me about a time you helped a teammate.
How do you give feedback in a code review?

question: What do you do when you disagree with a technical decision?
shortAnswer: First, I try to understand the other person's reasons. Then I explain my concern with facts, examples and trade-offs. If the team makes a different decision, I support it and help make it work. I only raise the issue again if new information shows a real risk.
detailedAnswer: A good answer is calm and practical. Show that you can disagree without creating conflict. For an important decision, suggest a small proof of concept, measurements or an ADR so the team can compare options.

followUps.question: Can you give an example of a trade-off?
When would you suggest a proof of concept?

question: How do you learn a new technology?
shortAnswer: I start with the basic concepts and the official documentation. Then I build a small project or proof of concept. After that, I use the technology in a real feature with tests and feedback. This is how I am growing with Kotlin, Kafka and Kubernetes.
detailedAnswer: This answer is stronger when you name your real learning process. A small proof of concept is useful because it answers a specific question, for example: Can this tool solve our problem? What are its limits? Do not say that you learn only by watching videos.
keyPoints: Build a small proof of concept

followUps.question: What is a recent technology you studied?
What question would your proof of concept answer?

question: How do you handle pressure or changing priorities?
shortAnswer: I first confirm what is most important and what can wait. I break the work into small steps and share risks early. If there is a production problem, I focus on a safe solution first, then I help find the root cause. Clear communication is important when priorities change.
detailedAnswer: Do not say that pressure never affects you. Show a simple process: confirm priority, make the work visible, communicate risk, solve the urgent problem safely, then improve the system after the incident.

followUps.question: What would you do if two urgent tasks arrive together?
How do you share a delivery risk early?

question: What are you looking for in your next role?
shortAnswer: I am looking for a team with real technical challenges, clear collaboration and room to learn. I want to use my Java and integration experience while growing in modern JVM and distributed-system tools. I also value a team that cares about quality and production reliability.
detailedAnswer: Keep this positive. Describe the kind of work and team where you do your best. Do not use this answer to complain about a past employer or to make demands before you understand the role.
redFlags: Talking only about salary or remote work

followUps.question: What helps you do your best work?
What do you value in a technical team?

question: Do you have any final questions for us?
shortAnswer: Yes. What would a successful first six months look like? What is the biggest technical or business challenge for the team now? And how do developers, product people and operations work together?
detailedAnswer: This is one of the most common final questions. Prepare three questions, but ask only the ones that were not answered. A good final question shows interest in the work, the team and how success is measured.

followUps.question: Which question would help you decide if the role is a fit?
How would you close the interview professionally?
